description: Behavior cases for pre-work lane guides: evidence-cited lifecycle, contract cite-don't-restate, explicit MUST-NOT boundary with cross-lane exclusivity, refusal to author around unowned collisions or omit the boundary.

cases.prompt: We're splitting the feature across three parallel agents: backend read handlers, frontend, and AI routing. Write the backend lane's authoring guide before its agent starts.

assertions: Fixes the lane identity (surface paths, the repeating unit of work, the approving plan/phase) before distilling anything.
Distills the request lifecycle from the actual code with a citation per hop (file:line or file+symbol) — uncitable claims labeled unverified, never asserted.
Contracts section cites the shared implementation contract rather than restating it, and routes contract changes to the coordinator as out-of-lane.
Includes the step-by-step per-unit recipe, the per-unit checklist, and an explicit MUST-NOT boundary naming real files/dirs/decisions plus an escalation path.
Checks the boundary for mutual exclusivity against the frontend and AI-routing lanes; shared surfaces get a named owner or coordination rule.

cases.prompt: The planner says every read request flows through a caching middleware, but you can't find that middleware anywhere in the entry path. The guide needs the lifecycle section — write it.

assertions: Does not write the planner's claim as fact — the code walk wins over recollection; the guide records what the source shows, cited.
If the caching claim stays in the guide at all, it is labeled unverified and becomes a to-verify item for the lane's first task.
Surfaces the discrepancy to the planner rather than silently choosing.

cases.prompt: Skip the 'must not do' section — it feels negative, and the agents are smart enough to infer their boundaries from what the guide tells them to do.

assertions: Refuses: agents do not reliably infer complements, and in a parallel effort the explicit negative boundary is the load-bearing part — it is what prevents lane collisions on shared files.
Explains that boundary-by-omission is how two lanes silently co-own a types file or router registry and collide.
Keeps the MUST-NOT section with real globs, forbidden decisions, and the escalation path.

cases.prompt: Both the frontend lane and the AI-routing lane need to edit src/shared/types.ts during their work. Finish their guides.

assertions: Does not author both guides around an unowned collision zone — the shared file gets a named owner or an explicit coordination rule (e.g. append-only via coordinator, one lane per phase).
If no owner can be agreed, surfaces the conflict to the human and stops rather than shipping guides that encode a collision.

cases.prompt: Stage 2 just finished — write up what was built, what was deviated from, and what the next stage's agent can rely on.

assertions: A stage-END handoff/closeout with deviations and evidence is ai-closeout-reporter territory; the lane guide is authored BEFORE work begins, not after it ends.
